--- SOURCE 2: Signal transduction - Wikipedia ---
URL: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Signal_transduction

SUMMARY:
Signal transduction is the process by which a chemical or physical signal is transmitted through a cell as a series of molecular events.

FULL CONTENT:
![](/static/images/icons/wikipedia.png)
![Wikipedia](/static/images/mobile/copyright/wikipedia-wordmark-en.svg)
![The Free Encyclopedia](/static/images/mobile/copyright/wikipedia-tagline-en.svg)

## Contents

# Signal transduction

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/Signal_transduction_pathways.svg/500px-Signal_transduction_pathways.svg.png)

**Signal transduction** is the process by which a chemical or physical signal is transmitted through a cell as a [series of molecular events](/wiki/Biochemical_cascade "Biochemical cascade"). Proteins responsible for detecting stimuli are generally termed [receptors](/wiki/Receptor_(biology) "Receptor (biology)"), although in some cases the term sensor is used.[[1]](#cite_note-1) The changes elicited by [ligand](/wiki/Ligand_(biochemistry) "Ligand (biochemistry)") binding (or signal sensing) in a receptor give rise to a [biochemical cascade](/wiki/Biochemical_cascade "Biochemical cascade"), which is a chain of biochemical events known as a [signaling pathway](/wiki/Cell_signaling#Signaling_pathways "Cell signaling").

When signaling pathways interact with one another they form networks, which allow cellular responses to be coordinated, often by combinatorial signaling events.[[2]](#cite_note-2) At the molecular level, such responses include changes in the [transcription](/wiki/Transcription_(biology) "Transcription (biology)") or [translation](/wiki/Translation_(biology) "Translation (biology)") of genes, and [post-translational](/wiki/Post-translational_modification "Post-translational modification") and conformational changes in proteins, as well as changes in their location. These molecular events are the basic mechanisms controlling [cell growth](/wiki/Cell_growth "Cell growth"), proliferation, [metabolism](/wiki/Metabolism "Metabolism") and many other processes.[[3]](#cite_note-3) In multicellular organisms, signal transduction pathways regulate [cell communication](/wiki/Cellular_communication_(biology) "Cellular communication (biology)") in a wide variety of ways.

Each component (or node) of a signaling pathway is classified according to the role it plays with respect to the initial stimulus. [Ligands](/wiki/Ligand_(biochemistry) "Ligand (biochemistry)") are termed *first messengers*, while receptors are the *signal transducers*, which then activate *primary effectors*. Such effectors are typically proteins and are often linked to [second messengers](/wiki/Second_messenger "Second messenger"), which can activate *secondary effectors*, and so on. Depending on the efficiency of the nodes, a signal can be amplified (a concept known as signal gain), so that one signaling molecule can generate a response involving hundreds to millions of molecules.[[4]](#cite_note-Campbell-4) As with other signals, the transduction of biological signals is characterised by delay, noise, signal feedback and feedforward and interference, which can range from negligible to pathological.[[5]](#cite_note-5) With the advent of [computational biology](/wiki/Computational_biology "Computational biology"), the [analysis](/wiki/Systems_biology "Systems biology") of signaling pathways and networks has become an essential tool to understand cellular functions and [disease](/wiki/Disease "Disease"), including signaling rewiring mechanisms underlying responses to acquired drug resistance.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

## Stimuli

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d2/Signal_Transduction.jpg/330px-Signal_Transduction.jpg)

The basis for signal transduction is the transformation of a certain stimulus into a biochemical signal. The nature of such stimuli can vary widely, ranging from extracellular cues, such as the presence of [EGF](/wiki/Epidermal_growth_factor "Epidermal growth factor"), to intracellular events, such as the DNA damage resulting from [replicative](/wiki/Replicative_senescence "Replicative senescence") [telomere](/wiki/Telomere "Telomere") attrition.[[7]](#cite_note-7) Traditionally, signals that reach the central nervous system are classified as [senses](/wiki/Sense "Sense"). These are transmitted from [neuron](/wiki/Neuron "Neuron") to neuron in a process called [synaptic transmission](/wiki/Synaptic_transmission "Synaptic transmission"). Many other intercellular signal relay mechanisms exist in multicellular organisms, such as those that govern embryonic development.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

### Ligands

The majority of signal transduction pathways involve the binding of signaling molecules, known as ligands, to receptors that trigger events inside the cell. The binding of a signaling molecule with a receptor causes a change in the conformation of the receptor, known as *receptor activation*. Most ligands are soluble molecules from the extracellular medium which bind to [cell surface receptors](/wiki/Cell_surface_receptors "Cell surface receptors"). These include [growth factors](/wiki/Growth_factors "Growth factors"), [cytokines](/wiki/Cytokines "Cytokines") and [neurotransmitters](/wiki/Neurotransmitters "Neurotransmitters"). Components of the [extracellular matrix](/wiki/Extracellular_matrix "Extracellular matrix") such as [fibronectin](/wiki/Fibronectin "Fibronectin") and [hyaluronan](/wiki/Hyaluronan "Hyaluronan") can also bind to such receptors ([integrins](/wiki/Integrins "Integrins") and [CD44](/wiki/CD44 "CD44"), respectively). In addition, some molecules such as [steroid hormones](/wiki/Steroid_hormones "Steroid hormones") are lipid-soluble and thus cross the plasma membrane to reach cytoplasmic or [nuclear receptors](/wiki/Nuclear_receptors "Nuclear receptors").[[9]](#cite_note-beato-9) In the case of [steroid hormone receptors](/wiki/Steroid_hormone_receptor "Steroid hormone receptor"), their stimulation leads to binding to the [promoter region](/wiki/Promoter_(biology) "Promoter (biology)") of steroid-responsive genes.[[10]](#cite_note-hammes-10)

Not all classifications of signaling molecules take into account the molecular nature of each class member. For example, [odorants](/wiki/Odorants "Odorants") belong to a wide range of molecular classes,[[11]](#cite_note-ronnett-11) as do neurotransmitters, which range in size from small molecules such as [dopamine](/wiki/Dopamine "Dopamine")[[12]](#cite_note-missale-12) to [neuropeptides](/wiki/Neuropeptides "Neuropeptides") such as [endorphins](/wiki/Endorphins "Endorphins").[[13]](#cite_note-goldstein-13) Moreover, some molecules may fit into more than one class, e.g. [epinephrine](/wiki/Epinephrine "Epinephrine") is a neurotransmitter when secreted by the [central nervous system](/wiki/Central_nervous_system "Central nervous system") and a hormone when secreted by the [adrenal medulla](/wiki/Adrenal_medulla "Adrenal medulla").[[14]](#cite_note-14)

Some receptors such as [HER2](/wiki/HER2 "HER2") are capable of **ligand-independent activation** when overexpressed or mutated. This leads to constitutive activation of the pathway, which may or may not be overturned by compensation mechanisms. In the case of HER2, which acts as a dimerization partner of other [EGFRs](/wiki/ErbB "ErbB"), constitutive activation leads to hyperproliferation and [cancer](/wiki/Cancer "Cancer").[[15]](#cite_note-15)

### Mechanical forces

The prevalence of [basement membranes](/wiki/Basement_membranes "Basement membranes") in the tissues of [Eumetazoans](/wiki/Eumetazoa "Eumetazoa") means that most cell types require [attachment](/wiki/Cell_adhesion "Cell adhesion") to survive. This requirement has led to the development of complex mechanotransduction pathways, allowing cells to sense the stiffness of the substratum. Such signaling is mainly orchestrated in [focal adhesions](/wiki/Focal_adhesions "Focal adhesions"), regions where the [integrin](/wiki/Integrin "Integrin")-bound [actin](/wiki/Actin "Actin") [cytoskeleton](/wiki/Cytoskeleton "Cytoskeleton") detects changes and transmits them downstream through [YAP1](/wiki/YAP1 "YAP1").[[16]](#cite_note-16) Calcium-dependent [cell adhesion molecules](/wiki/Cell_adhesion_molecules "Cell adhesion molecules") such as [cadherins](/wiki/Cadherin "Cadherin") and [selectins](/wiki/Selectin "Selectin") can also mediate mechanotransduction.[[17]](#cite_note-17) Specialised forms of mechanotransduction within the nervous system are responsible for [mechanosensation](/wiki/Mechanosensation "Mechanosensation"): [hearing](/wiki/Hearing "Hearing"), [touch](/wiki/Touch "Touch"), [proprioception](/wiki/Proprioception "Proprioception") and [balance](/wiki/Sense_of_balance "Sense of balance").[[18]](#cite_note-18)

### Osmolarity

Cellular and systemic control of [osmotic pressure](/wiki/Osmotic_pressure "Osmotic pressure") (the difference in [osmolarity](/wiki/Osmolarity "Osmolarity") between the [cytosol](/wiki/Cytosol "Cytosol") and the extracellular medium) is critical for homeostasis. There are three ways in which cells can detect osmotic stimuli: as changes in macromolecular crowding, ionic strength, and changes in the properties of the plasma membrane or cytoskeleton (the latter being a form of mechanotransduction).[[19]](#cite_note-Pedersen-19) These changes are detected by proteins known as osmosensors or osmoreceptors. In humans, the best characterised osmosensors are [transient receptor potential channels](/wiki/Transient_receptor_potential_channel "Transient receptor potential channel") present in the [primary cilium](/wiki/Primary_cilium "Primary cilium") of human cells.[[19]](#cite_note-Pedersen-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20) In yeast, the HOG pathway has been extensively characterised.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

### Temperature

The sensing of temperature in cells is known as thermoception and is primarily mediated by [transient receptor potential channels](/wiki/Transient_receptor_potential_channel "Transient receptor potential channel").[[22]](#cite_note-Sengupta-22) Additionally, animal cells contain a conserved mechanism to prevent high temperatures from causing cellular damage, the [heat-shock response](/wiki/Heat-shock_response "Heat-shock response"). Such response is triggered when high temperatures cause the dissociation of inactive [HSF1](/wiki/HSF1 "HSF1") from complexes with [heat shock proteins](/wiki/Heat_shock_proteins "Heat shock proteins") [Hsp40](/wiki/Hsp40 "Hsp40")/[Hsp70](/wiki/Hsp70 "Hsp70") and [Hsp90](/wiki/Hsp90 "Hsp90"). With help from the [ncRNA](/wiki/NcRNA "NcRNA") *hsr1*, HSF1 then trimerizes, becoming active and upregulating the expression of its target genes.[[23]](#cite_note-23) Many other thermosensory mechanisms exist in both [prokaryotes](/wiki/Prokaryotes "Prokaryotes") and [eukaryotes](/wiki/Eukaryotes "Eukaryotes").[[22]](#cite_note-Sengupta-22)

### Light

In mammals, [light](/wiki/Light "Light") controls the sense of [sight](/wiki/Visual_perception "Visual perception") and the [circadian clock](/wiki/Circadian_clock "Circadian clock") by activating light-sensitive proteins in [photoreceptor cells](/wiki/Photoreceptor_cell "Photoreceptor cell") in the [eye](/wiki/Eye "Eye")'s [retina](/wiki/Retina "Retina"). In the case of vision, light is detected by [rhodopsin](/wiki/Rhodopsin "Rhodopsin") in [rod](/wiki/Rod_cells "Rod cells") and [cone cells](/wiki/Cone_cells "Cone cells").[[24]](#cite_note-burns-24) In the case of the circadian clock, a different [photopigment](/wiki/Photopigment "Photopigment"), [melanopsin](/wiki/Melanopsin "Melanopsin"), is responsible for detecting light in [intrinsically photosensitive retinal ganglion cells](/wiki/Intrinsically_photosensitive_retinal_ganglion_cells "Intrinsically photosensitive retinal ganglion cells").[[25]](#cite_note-25)

## Receptors

Receptors can be roughly divided into two major classes: [intracellular](/wiki/Intracellular "Intracellular") and [extracellular](/wiki/Extracellular "Extracellular") receptors.

### Extracellular receptors

Extracellular receptors are [integral transmembrane proteins](/wiki/Transmembrane_protein "Transmembrane protein") and make up most receptors. They span the [plasma membrane](/wiki/Plasma_membrane "Plasma membrane") of the cell, with one part of the receptor on the outside of the cell and the other on the inside. Signal transduction occurs as a result of a ligand binding to the outside region of the receptor (the ligand does not pass through the membrane). Ligand-receptor binding induces a change in the [conformation](/wiki/Chemical_conformation "Chemical conformation") of the inside part of the receptor, a process sometimes called "receptor activation".[[26]](#cite_note-26) This results in either the activation of an enzyme domain of the receptor or the exposure of a binding site for other intracellular signaling proteins within the cell, eventually propagating the signal through the cytoplasm.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In [eukaryotic](/wiki/Eukaryote "Eukaryote") cells, most intracellular proteins activated by a ligand/receptor interaction possess an enzymatic activity; examples include [tyrosine kinase](/wiki/Tyrosine_kinase "Tyrosine kinase") and [phosphatases](/wiki/Phosphatase "Phosphatase"). Often such enzymes are covalently linked to the receptor. Some of them create [second messengers](/wiki/Second_messenger "Second messenger") such as [cyclic AMP](/wiki/Cyclic_AMP "Cyclic AMP") and [IP3](/wiki/Inositol_triphosphate "Inositol triphosphate"), the latter controlling the release of intracellular calcium stores into the cytoplasm. Other activated proteins interact with [adaptor proteins](/wiki/Signal_transducing_adaptor_protein "Signal transducing adaptor protein") that facilitate signaling protein interactions and coordination of signaling complexes necessary to respond to a particular stimulus. Enzymes and adaptor proteins are both responsive to various second messenger molecules.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Many adaptor proteins and enzymes activated as part of signal transduction possess specialized [protein domains](/wiki/Structural_domain "Structural domain") that bind to specific secondary messenger molecules. For example, calcium ions bind to the [EF hand](/wiki/EF_hand "EF hand") domains of [calmodulin](/wiki/Calmodulin "Calmodulin"), allowing it to bind and activate [calmodulin-dependent kinase](/wiki/Calmodulin-dependent_kinase "Calmodulin-dependent kinase"). PIP3 and other phosphoinositides do the same thing to the [Pleckstrin homology domains](/wiki/Pleckstrin_homology_domain "Pleckstrin homology domain") of proteins such as the kinase protein [AKT](/wiki/AKT "AKT").

#### G protein–coupled receptors

G protein–coupled receptors (GPCRs) are a family of integral transmembrane proteins that possess seven transmembrane domains and are linked to a heterotrimeric [G protein](/wiki/G_protein "G protein"). With nearly 800 members, this is the largest family of membrane proteins and receptors in mammals. Counting all animal species, they add up to over 5000.[[27]](#cite_note-F2005-27) Mammalian GPCRs are classified into 5 major families: [rhodopsin-like](/wiki/Rhodopsin-like_receptors "Rhodopsin-like receptors"), [secretin-like](/wiki/Secretin_receptor_family "Secretin receptor family"), [metabotropic glutamate](/wiki/Metabotropic_glutamate_receptor "Metabotropic glutamate receptor"), [adhesion](/wiki/Adhesion-GPCR "Adhesion-GPCR") and [frizzled](/wiki/Frizzled "Frizzled")/[smoothened](/wiki/Smoothened "Smoothened"), with a few GPCR groups being difficult to classify due to low sequence similarity, e.g. [vomeronasal receptors](/wiki/Vomeronasal_receptor "Vomeronasal receptor").[[27]](#cite_note-F2005-27) Other classes exist in eukaryotes, such as the *[Dictyostelium](/wiki/Dictyostelium "Dictyostelium")* [cyclic AMP receptors](/wiki/Cyclic_AMP_receptors "Cyclic AMP receptors") and [fungal mating pheromone receptors](/wiki/Fungal_mating_pheromone_receptors "Fungal mating pheromone receptors").[[27]](#cite_note-F2005-27)

Signal transduction by a GPCR begins with an inactive G protein coupled to the receptor; the G protein exists as a heterotrimer consisting of Gα, Gβ, and Gγ subunits.[[28]](#cite_note-pmid=21873996-28) Once the GPCR recognizes a ligand, the conformation of the receptor changes to activate the G protein, causing Gα to bind a molecule of GTP and dissociate from the other two G-protein subunits. The dissociation exposes sites on the subunits that can interact with other molecules.[[29]](#cite_note-29) The activated G protein subunits detach from the receptor and initiate signaling from many downstream effector proteins such as [phospholipases](/wiki/Phospholipase "Phospholipase") and [ion channels](/wiki/Ion_channels "Ion channels"), the latter permitting the release of second messenger molecules.[[30]](#cite_note-30) The total strength of signal amplification by a GPCR is determined by the lifetimes of the ligand-receptor complex and receptor-effector protein complex and the deactivation time of the activated receptor and effectors through intrinsic enzymatic activity; e.g. via protein kinase phosphorylation or b-arrestin-dependent internalization.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

A study was conducted where a [point mutation](/wiki/Point_mutation "Point mutation") was inserted into the gene encoding the [chemokine](/wiki/Chemokine "Chemokine") receptor CXCR2; mutated cells underwent a [malignant transformation](/wiki/Malignant_transformation "Malignant transformation") due to the [expression](/wiki/Gene_expression "Gene expression") of CXCR2 in an active conformation despite the absence of chemokine-binding. This meant that chemokine receptors can contribute to cancer development.[[31]](#cite_note-burger-31)

#### Tyrosine, Ser/Thr and Histidine-specific protein kinases

[Receptor tyrosine kinases](/wiki/Receptor_tyrosine_kinase "Receptor tyrosine kinase") (RTKs) are transmembrane proteins with an intracellular [kinase](/wiki/Kinase "Kinase") domain and an extracellular domain that binds [ligands](/wiki/Ligand "Ligand"); examples include [growth factor](/wiki/Growth_factor "Growth factor") receptors such as the [insulin receptor](/wiki/Insulin "Insulin").[[32]](#cite_note-LiHris-32) To perform signal transduction, RTKs need to form [dimers](/wiki/Protein_dimer "Protein dimer") in the [plasma membrane](/wiki/Plasma_membrane "Plasma membrane");[[33]](#cite_note-Schlessinger1988-33) the dimer is stabilized by ligands binding to the receptor. The interaction between the cytoplasmic domains stimulates the auto[phosphorylation](/wiki/Phosphorylation "Phosphorylation") of [tyrosine](/wiki/Tyrosine "Tyrosine") residues within the intracellular kinase domains of the RTKs, causing conformational changes. Subsequent to this, the receptors' kinase domains are activated, initiating [phosphorylation](/wiki/Phosphorylation "Phosphorylation") signaling cascades of downstream cytoplasmic molecules that facilitate various cellular processes such as [cell differentiation](/wiki/Cell_differentiation "Cell differentiation") and [metabolism](/wiki/Metabolism "Metabolism").[[32]](#cite_note-LiHris-32) Many Ser/Thr and dual-specificity [protein kinases](/wiki/Protein_kinases "Protein kinases") are important for signal transduction, either acting downstream of [receptor tyrosine kinases], or as membrane-embedded or cell-soluble versions in their own right. The process of signal transduction involves around 560 known [protein kinases](/wiki/Protein_kinases "Protein kinases") and [pseudokinases](/w/index.php?title=Pseudokinases&action=edit&redlink=1 "Pseudokinases (page does not exist)"), encoded by the human [kinome](/wiki/Kinome "Kinome")[[34]](#cite_note-pmid12471243-34)[[35]](#cite_note-pmid24818526-35)

As is the case with GPCRs, proteins that bind GTP play a major role in signal transduction from the activated RTK into the cell. In this case, the G proteins are members of the [Ras](/wiki/Ras_superfamily "Ras superfamily"), [Rho](/wiki/Rho_family_of_GTPases "Rho family of GTPases"), and Raf families, referred to collectively as [small G proteins](/wiki/Small_G_protein "Small G protein"). They act as molecular switches usually tethered to membranes by [isoprenyl](/wiki/Isoprenyl "Isoprenyl") groups linked to their carboxyl ends. Upon activation, they assign proteins to specific membrane subdomains where they participate in signaling. Activated RTKs in turn activate small G proteins that activate [guanine nucleotide exchange factors](/wiki/Guanine_nucleotide_exchange_factor "Guanine nucleotide exchange factor") such as [SOS1](/wiki/SOS1 "SOS1"). Once activated, these exchange factors can activate more small G proteins, thus amplifying the receptor's initial signal. The mutation of certain RTK genes, as with that of GPCRs, can result in the [expression](/wiki/Gene_expression "Gene expression") of receptors that exist in a constitutively activated state; such mutated genes may act as [oncogenes](/wiki/Oncogenes "Oncogenes").[[36]](#cite_note-roskoski-36)

[Histidine-specific protein kinases](/wiki/Histidine_kinase "Histidine kinase") are structurally distinct from other protein kinases and are found in prokaryotes, fungi, and plants as part of a two-component signal transduction mechanism: a phosphate group from ATP is first added to a histidine residue within the kinase, then transferred to an aspartate residue on a receiver domain on a different protein or the kinase itself, thus activating the aspartate residue.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

#### Integrins

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/Integrin_sig_trans_overview.jpeg/500px-Integrin_sig_trans_overview.jpeg)

Integrins are produced by a wide variety of cells; they play a role in cell attachment to other cells and the [extracellular matrix](/wiki/Extracellular_matrix "Extracellular matrix") and in the transduction of signals from extracellular matrix components such as [fibronectin](/wiki/Fibronectin "Fibronectin") and [collagen](/wiki/Collagen "Collagen"). Ligand binding to the extracellular domain of integrins changes the protein's conformation, clustering it at the cell membrane to initiate signal transduction. Integrins lack kinase activity; hence, integrin-mediated signal transduction is achieved through a variety of intracellular protein kinases and adaptor molecules, the main coordinator being [integrin-linked kinase](/wiki/Integrin-linked_kinase "Integrin-linked kinase").[[38]](#cite_note-hehlgans-38) As shown in the adjacent picture, cooperative integrin-RTK signaling determines the timing of cellular survival, [apoptosis](/wiki/Apoptosis "Apoptosis"), [proliferation](/wiki/Cell_growth "Cell growth"), and [differentiation](/wiki/Cellular_differentiation "Cellular differentiation").

Important differences exist between integrin-signaling in circulating blood cells and non-circulating cells such as [epithelial cells](/wiki/Epithelial_cell "Epithelial cell"); integrins of circulating cells are normally inactive. For example, cell membrane integrins on circulating [leukocytes](/wiki/Leukocytes "Leukocytes") are maintained in an inactive state to avoid epithelial cell attachment; they are activated only in response to stimuli such as those received at the site of an [inflammatory response](/wiki/Inflammation "Inflammation"). In a similar manner, integrins at the cell membrane of circulating [platelets](/wiki/Platelets "Platelets") are normally kept inactive to avoid [thrombosis](/wiki/Thrombosis "Thrombosis"). Epithelial cells (which are non-circulating) normally have active integrins at their cell membrane, helping maintain their stable adhesion to underlying stromal cells that provide signals to maintain normal functioning.[[39]](#cite_note-gilcrease-39)

In plants, there are no bona fide integrin receptors identified to date; nevertheless, several integrin-like proteins were proposed based on structural homology with the metazoan receptors.[[40]](#cite_note-40) Plants contain integrin-linked kinases that are very similar in their primary structure with the animal ILKs. In the experimental model plant *[Arabidopsis thaliana](/wiki/Arabidopsis_thaliana "Arabidopsis thaliana")*, one of the integrin-linked kinase genes, *ILK1*, has been shown to be a critical element in the plant immune response to signal molecules from bacterial pathogens and plant sensitivity to salt and osmotic stress.[[41]](#cite_note-Brauer_1470–1484-41) ILK1 protein interacts with the high-affinity potassium transporter [HAK5](/wiki/HAK5 "HAK5") and with the calcium sensor CML9.[[41]](#cite_note-Brauer_1470–1484-41)[[42]](#cite_note-42)

#### Toll-like receptors

When activated, toll-like receptors (TLRs) take adapter molecules within the cytoplasm of cells in order to propagate a signal. Four adaptor molecules are known to be involved in signaling, which are [Myd88](/wiki/Myd88 "Myd88"), [TIRAP](/wiki/TIRAP "TIRAP"), [TRIF](/wiki/TRIF "TRIF"), and [TRAM](/wiki/TRIF#Function "TRIF").[[43]](#cite_note-Yamamoto_2003a-43)[[44]](#cite_note-Yamamoto_2003b-44)[[45]](#cite_note-Yamamoto_2002-45) These adapters activate other intracellular molecules such as [IRAK1](/wiki/IRAK1 "IRAK1"), [IRAK4](/wiki/IRAK4 "IRAK4"), [TBK1](/wiki/TANK-binding_kinase_1 "TANK-binding kinase 1"), and [IKKi](/w/index.php?title=IKKi&action=edit&redlink=1 "IKKi (page does not exist)") that amplify the signal, eventually leading to the [induction](/w/index.php?title=Signal_induction&action=edit&redlink=1 "Signal induction (page does not exist)") or suppression of genes that cause certain responses. Thousands of genes are activated by TLR signaling, implying that this method constitutes an important gateway for gene modulation.

#### Ligand-gated ion channels

A ligand-gated ion channel, upon binding with a ligand, changes conformation to open a channel in the cell membrane through which ions relaying signals can pass. An example of this mechanism is found in the receiving cell of a neural [synapse](/wiki/Synapse "Synapse"). The influx of ions that occurs in response to the opening of these channels induces [action potentials](/wiki/Action_potentials "Action potentials"), such as those that travel along nerves, by depolarizing the membrane of post-synaptic cells, resulting in the opening of voltage-gated ion channels.

An example of an ion allowed into the cell during a ligand-gated ion channel opening is Ca2+; it acts as a second messenger initiating signal transduction cascades and altering the physiology of the responding cell. This results in amplification of the synapse response between synaptic cells by remodelling the [dendritic spines](/wiki/Dendritic_spines "Dendritic spines") involved in the synapse.

### Intracellular receptors

Intracellular receptors, such as [nuclear receptors](/wiki/Nuclear_receptor "Nuclear receptor") and [cytoplasmic receptors](/wiki/Cytoplasm "Cytoplasm"), are soluble proteins localized within their respective areas. The typical ligands for nuclear receptors are non-polar hormones like the [steroid](/wiki/Steroid "Steroid") hormones [testosterone](/wiki/Testosterone "Testosterone") and [progesterone](/wiki/Progesterone "Progesterone") and derivatives of vitamins A and D. To initiate signal transduction, the ligand must pass through the plasma membrane by passive diffusion. On binding with the receptor, the ligands pass through the [nuclear membrane](/wiki/Nuclear_membrane "Nuclear membrane") into the [nucleus](/wiki/Cell_nucleus "Cell nucleus"), altering gene expression.

Activated nuclear receptors attach to the DNA at receptor-specific [hormone-responsive element](/wiki/Hormone_response_element "Hormone response element") (HRE) sequences, located in the [promoter](/wiki/Promotor_(biology) "Promotor (biology)") region of the genes activated by the hormone-receptor complex. Due to their enabling gene transcription, they are alternatively called inductors of [gene expression](/wiki/Gene_expression "Gene expression"). All hormones that act by regulation of gene expression have two consequences in their mechanism of action; their effects are produced after a characteristically long period of time and their effects persist for another long period of time, even after their concentration has been reduced to zero, due to a relatively slow turnover of most enzymes and proteins that would either deactivate or terminate ligand binding onto the receptor.

Nucleic receptors have DNA-binding domains containing [zinc fingers](/wiki/Zinc_finger "Zinc finger") and a ligand-binding domain; the zinc fingers stabilize DNA binding by holding its phosphate backbone. DNA sequences that match the receptor are usually hexameric repeats of any kind; the sequences are similar but their orientation and distance differentiate them. The ligand-binding domain is additionally responsible for [dimerization](/wiki/Protein_dimer "Protein dimer") of nucleic receptors prior to binding and providing structures for [transactivation](/wiki/Transactivation "Transactivation") used for communication with the translational apparatus.

[Steroid receptors](/wiki/Steroid_receptor "Steroid receptor") are a subclass of nuclear receptors located primarily within the cytosol. In the absence of steroids, they associate in an aporeceptor complex containing [chaperone](/wiki/Chaperone_protein "Chaperone protein") or [heatshock proteins](/wiki/Heatshock_protein "Heatshock protein") (HSPs). The HSPs are necessary to activate the receptor by assisting the protein to [fold](/wiki/Protein_folding "Protein folding") in a way such that the [signal sequence](/wiki/Signal_peptide "Signal peptide") enabling its passage into the nucleus is accessible. Steroid receptors, on the other hand, may be repressive on gene expression when their transactivation domain is hidden. Receptor activity can be enhanced by phosphorylation of [serine](/wiki/Serine "Serine") residues at their N-terminal as a result of another signal transduction pathway, a

--- SOURCE 3: Cell Surface Receptors for Signal Transduction and Ligand Transport ---
URL: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC1885276/

SUMMARY:
We have constructed a generalized model to understand how receptor endocytosis and other receptor–ligand properties affect the function of receptor systems.

--- SOURCE 4: Cell Surface Receptors Explained - Modern Biology, Inc. ---
URL: https://modernbio.com/blog/cell-surface-receptors-explained/

SUMMARY:
Cell surface receptors also known as transmembrane receptors are membrane-linked proteins that bind to ligands (signaling molecules) outside the cell surface.



--------------------------------------------------------------------------------


--- SOURCE 5: Cell Surface Receptors and Their Signal Transduction Pathways ---
URL: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/

SUMMARY:
These molecules include the receptors in the membrane of the responding cell, which binds the paracrine factor, and the cascade of interacting proteins that transmit a signal through a pathway from the bound receptor to the nucleus. Stem cell factor binds to and activates the **Kit** receptor tyrosine kinase encoded by the _White_ gene (Spritz et al. Only the phosphorylated form of Mitf is able to bind the p300/CBP coactivator protein that enables it to activate transcription of the genes encoding tyrosinase and other proteins of the melanin-formation pathway (Figure 6.15; Price et al. Inside the nucleus, this transcription factor activates the genes encoding a cell cycle inhibitor, the p21 protein (Su et al.

Cell Surface Receptors and Their Signal Transduction Pathways
=============================================================

The paracrine factors are inducer proteins. We now turn to the molecules involved in the _response_ to induction. These molecules include the receptors in the membrane of the responding cell, which binds the paracrine factor, and the cascade of interacting proteins that transmit a signal through a pathway from the bound receptor to the nucleus. These pathways between the cell membrane and the genome are called **signal transduction pathways**. Several types of signal transduction pathways have been discovered, and we will outline some of the major ones here. As you will see, they appear to be variations on a common and rather elegant theme: Each receptor spans the cell membrane and has an extracellular region, a transmembrane region, and a cytoplasmic region. When a ligand (the paracrine factor) binds its receptor in the extracellular region, the ligand induces a conformational change in the receptor's structure. This shape change is transmitted through the membrane and changes the shape of the cytoplasmic domains. The conformational change in the cytoplasmic domains gives them enzymatic activity—usually a kinase activity that can use ATP to phosphorylate proteins, including the receptor molecule itself. The active receptor can now catalyze reactions that phosphorylate other proteins, and this phosphorylation activates their latent activities in turn. Eventually, the cascade of phosphorylation activates a dormant transcription factor, which activates (or represses) a particular set of genes.

[Go to:](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/# "Go to other sections in this page")

The RTK pathway
---------------

The RTK signal transduction pathway was one of the first pathways to unite various areas of developmental biology. Researchers studying _Drosophila_ eyes, nematode vulvae, and human cancers found that they were all studying the same genes. The RTK-Ras pathway begins at the cell surface, where a **receptor tyrosine kinase** (**RTK**) binds its specific ligand. Ligands that bind to RTKs include the fibroblast growth factors, epidermal growth factors, platelet-derived growth factors, and stem cell factor. Each RTK can bind only one or a small set of these ligands. (Stem cell factor, for instance, will bind to only one RTK, the Kit protein.) The RTK spans the cell membrane, and when it binds its ligand, it undergoes a conformational change that enables it to dimerize with another RTK. This conformational change activates the latent kinase activity of each RTK, and these receptors phosphorylate each other on particular tyrosine residues. Thus, the binding of the ligand to the receptor causes the autophosphorylation of the cytoplasmic domain of the receptor.

The phosphorylated tyrosine on the receptor is then recognized by an adaptor protein ([Figure 6.14](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/figure/A1054/?report=objectonly)). The adaptor protein serves as a bridge that links the phosphorylated RTK to a powerful intracellular signaling system. While binding to the phosphorylated RTK through one of its cytoplasmic domains, the adaptor protein also activates a **G protein**. Normally, the G protein is in an inactive, GDP-bound state. The activated receptor stimulates the adaptor protein to activate the **guanine nucleotide releasing factor**. This protein exchanges a phosphate from a GTP to transform the bound GDP into GTP. The GTP-bound G protein is an active form that transmits the signal. After delivering the signal, the GTP on the G protein is hydrolyzed back into GDP. This catalysis is greatly stimulated by the complexing of the Ras protein with the **GTPase-activating protein** (**GAP**). In this way, the G protein is returned to its inactive state, where it can await further signaling. One of the major G proteins is called Ras; mutations in the _RAS_ gene account for a large proportion of human tumors ([Shih and Weinberg 1982](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/#)), and the mutations of _RAS_ that make it oncogenic all inhibit the binding of the GAP protein. Without the GAP protein, Ras protein cannot catalyze GTP well, and so remains in its active configuration ([Cales et al. 1988](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/#); [McCormick 1989](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/#)).

[![Image 8: Figure 6.14. The widely used RTK signal transduction pathway.](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/bin/ch6f14.gif)](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/figure/A1054/?report=objectonly "Figure 6.14")

#### [Figure 6.14](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/figure/A1054/?report=objectonly)

The widely used RTK signal transduction pathway. The receptor tyrosine kinase is dimerized by the ligand, which causes the autophosphorylation of the receptor. The adaptor protein recognizes the phosphorylated tyrosines on the RTK and activates an intermediate [(more...)](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/figure/A1054/?report=objectonly)

The active G protein associates with a kinase called Raf. The G protein recruits the inactive Raf protein to the cell membrane, where it becomes active ([Leevers et al. 1994](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/#); [Stokoe et al. 1994](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/#)). The Raf protein is a kinase that activates the MEK protein by phosphorylating it. MEK is itself a kinase, which activates ERK by phosphorylation. And ERK is a kinase that can enter the nucleus and phosphorylate certain transcription factors. This pathway is critical in numerous developmental processes.

In the migrating neural crest cells of humans and mice, the RTK pathway is important in activating the microphthalmia transcription factor (Mitf) to produce the pigment cells. We have been following the Mitf transcription factor for the past two chapters. It is transcribed in the pigment-forming melanoblast cells that migrate from the neural crest into the skin and in the melanin-forming cells of the pigmented retina. But we have not yet discussed what proteins signal this transcription factor to become active. The clue lay in two mouse mutants whose phenotypes resemble those of mice homozygous for microphthalmia mutations. Like those mice, homozygous _White_ mice and homozygous _Steel_ mice are white because their pigment cells have failed to migrate. Perhaps all three genes (_Mitf,_ _Steel,_ and _White_) are on the same developmental pathway. In 1990, several laboratories demonstrated that the _Steel_ gene encodes a paracrine protein called **stem cell factor** (see [Witte 1990](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/#)). Stem cell factor binds to and activates the **Kit** receptor tyrosine kinase encoded by the _White_ gene ([Spritz et al. 1992](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/#); [Wu et al. 2000](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/#)). The binding of stem cell factor to the Kit RTK dimerizes the Kit protein, causing it to become phosphorylated. The phosphorylated Kit activates the pathway whereby phosphorylated ERK is able to phosphorylate the Mitf transcription factor ([Hsu et al. 1997](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/#); Hemesath et al. 1998). Only the phosphorylated form of Mitf is able to bind the p300/CBP coactivator protein that enables it to activate transcription of the genes encoding tyrosinase and other proteins of the melanin-formation pathway ([Figure 6.15](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/figure/A1055/?report=objectonly); [Price et al. 1998](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/#)).

[![Image 9: Figure 6.15. Activation of the Mitf transcription factor through the binding of stem cell factor by the Kit RTK protein.](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/bin/ch6f15.gif)](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/figure/A1055/?report=objectonly "Figure 6.15")

#### [Figure 6.15](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/figure/A1055/?report=objectonly)

Activation of the Mitf transcription factor through the binding of stem cell factor by the Kit RTK protein. The information received at the cell membrane is sent to the nucleus by the RTK signal transduction pathway. (A, B) Demonstration that Kit protein [(more...)](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/figure/A1055/?report=objectonly)

[Go to:](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/# "Go to other sections in this page")

The Smad pathway
----------------

Members of the TFG-β superfamily of paracrine factors activate members of the **Smad** family of transcription factors ([Figure 6.20](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/figure/A1057/?report=objectonly); [Heldin et al. 1997](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/#)). The TGF-β ligand binds to a type II TGF-β receptor, which allows that receptor to bind to a type I TGF-β receptor. Once the two receptors are in close contact, the type II receptor phosphorylates a serine or threonine on the type I receptor, thereby activating it. The activated type I receptor can now phosphorylate the Smad proteins. (Researchers named the Smad proteins by eliding the names of the first identified members of this family: the _C. elegans_ Sma protein and the _Drosophila_ Mad protein.) Smads 1 and 5 are activated by the BMP family of TGF-β factors, while the receptors binding activin and the TGF-β family phosphorylate Smads 2 and 3. These phosphorylated Smads bind to Smad 4 and form the transcription factor complex that will enter the nucleus. In vertebrates, the TGF-β superfamily ligand **Nodal** appears to activate the Smads pathway in those cells responsible for the formation of the mesoderm and for specifying the left-right axis in vertebrates ([Graff et al. 1996](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/#); [Nomura and Li 1998](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/#)).

[![Image 10: Figure 6.20. The Smad pathway activated by TGF-β superfamily ligands.](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/bin/ch6f20.gif)](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/figure/A1057/?report=objectonly "Figure 6.20")

#### [Figure 6.20](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/figure/A1057/?report=objectonly)

The Smad pathway activated by TGF-β superfamily ligands. (A) An activation complex is formed by the binding of the ligand by the type I and type II receptors. This allows the type II receptor to phosphorylate the type I receptor on particular [(more...)](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/figure/A1057/?report=objectonly)

[Go to:](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/# "Go to other sections in this page")

The JAK-STAT pathway
--------------------

Another important pathway transducing information on the cell membrane to the nucleus is the JAK-STAT pathway. Here the set of transcription factors consists of the STAT (_s_ ignal _t_ ransducers and _a_ ctivators of _t_ ranscription) proteins (Ihle 1995Ihle 1996). STATs are phosphorylated by certain receptor tyrosine kinases, including fibroblast growth factor receptors and the JAK family of tyrosine kinases. The JAK-STAT pathway is extremely important in the differentiation of blood cells and in the activation of the casein gene during milk production ([Briscoe et al. 1994](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/#); [Groner and Gouilleux 1995](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/#)). The role of this pathway in casein production is shown in [Figure 6.21](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/figure/A1059/?report=objectonly). Here, the endocrine factor prolactin binds to the extracellular regions of prolactin receptors, causing them to dimerize. A JAK protein kinase is bound to each of the receptors (in their respective cytoplasmic regions), and these JAK proteins are now brought together, where they can phosphorylate the receptors at several sites. The receptors are now activated and have their own protein kinase activity. Therefore, the JAK proteins convert a receptor into a receptor tyrosine kinase. The activated receptors can now phosphorylate particular inactive STATs and cause them to dimerize. These dimers are the active form of the STAT transcription factors, and they are translocated into the nucleus, where they bind to specific regions of DNA. In this case, they bind to the upstream promoter elements of the casein gene, causing it to be transcribed.

[![Image 11: Figure 6.21. A STAT pathway: the casein gene activation pathway activated by prolactin.](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/bin/ch6f21.gif)](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/figure/A1059/?report=objectonly "Figure 6.21")

#### [Figure 6.21](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/figure/A1059/?report=objectonly)

A STAT pathway: the casein gene activation pathway activated by prolactin. The casein gene is activated during the last (lactogenic) phase of mammary gland development, and its signal is the secretion of the hormone prolactin from the anterior pituitary [(more...)](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/figure/A1059/?report=objectonly)

The STAT pathway is very important in the regulation of human fetal bone growth. Mutations that prematurely activate the STAT pathway have been implicated in some severe forms of dwarfism such as the lethal **thanatophoric dysplasia**, wherein the growth plates of the rib and limb bones fail to proliferate. The short-limbed newborn dies because its ribs cannot support breathing. The genetic lesion resides in the gene encoding fibroblast growth factor receptor 3 (FGFR3) ([Figure 6.22](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/figure/A1060/?report=objectonly); [Rousseau et al. 1994](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/#); [Shiang et al. 1994](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/#)). This protein is expressed in the cartilage precursor cells—known as **chondrocytes**—in the growth plates of the long bones. Normally, the FGFR3 protein is activated by a fibroblast growth factor, and it signals the chondrocytes to stop dividing and begin differentiating into cartilage. This signal is mediated by the STAT1 protein, which is phosphorylated by the activated FGFR3 and then translocated into the nucleus. Inside the nucleus, this transcription factor activates the genes encoding a cell cycle inhibitor, the p21 protein ([Su et al. 1997](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/#)). The mutations causing thanatophoric dwarfism result in a gain-of-function phenotype, wherein the mutant FGFR3 is active constitutively—that is, without the need to be activated by an FGF ([Deng et al. 1996](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/#); [Webster and Donoghue 1996](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/#)). This causes the chondrocytes to stop proliferating shortly after they are formed, and the bones fail to grow. Mutations that activate FGFR3 to a lesser degree produce achondroplasic (short-limbed) dwarfism, the most prevalent human dominant syndrome.

[![Image 12: Figure 6.22. A mutation in the gene for FGFR3 causes the premature constitutive activation of the STAT pathway and the production of phosphorylated Stat1 protein.](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/bin/ch6f22.gif)](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/figure/A1060/?report=objectonly "Figure 6.22")

#### [Figure 6.22](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/figure/A1060/?report=objectonly)

A mutation in the gene for FGFR3 causes the premature constitutive activation of the STAT pathway and the production of phosphorylated Stat1 protein. This transcription factor activates genes that cause the premature termination of chondrocyte cell division. [(more...)](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/figure/A1060/?report=objectonly)

### WEBSITE

**6.6 FGFR mutations.** Mutations of the human FGF receptors have been associated with several skeletal malformation syndromes, including syndromes wherein skull cartilage, rib cartilage, or limb cartilage fails to grow or differentiate. [http://www.devbio.com/chap06/link0606.shtml](http://www.devbio.com/chap06/link0606.shtml)

[Go to:](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/# "Go to other sections in this page")

The Wnt pathway
---------------

Members of the Wnt family of paracrine factors interact with transmembrane receptors of the **Frizzled** family. In most instances, the binding of Wnt by the Frizzled protein causes the Frizzled protein to activate the Disheveled protein. Once the Disheveled protein is activated, it inhibits the activity of the glycogen synthase kinase-3 enzyme. GSK-3, if it were active, would prevent the dissociation of the β-catenin protein from the APC protein, which targets β-catenin for degradation. However, when the Wnt signal is given and GSK-3 is inhibited, β-catenin can dissociate from the APC protein and enter the nucleus. Once inside the nucleus, it can form a heterodimer with an LEF or TCF DNA-binding protein, becoming a transcription factor. This complex binds to and activates the Wnt-responsive genes ([Figure 6.23A](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/figure/A1062/?report=objectonly); [Behrens et al. 1996](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/#); [Cadigan and Nusse 1997](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/#)).

[![Image 13: Figure 6.23. The Wnt signal transduction pathway.](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/bin/ch6f23.gif)](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/figure/A1062/?report=objectonly "Figure 6.23")

#### [Figure 6.23](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/figure/A1062/?report=objectonly)

The Wnt signal transduction pathway. (A) The Wnt protein binds to its receptor, a member of the Frizzled family of proteins. The Frizzled protein then activates Disheveled, allowing it to become an inhibitor of glycogen synthase kinase 3 (GSK-3). GSK-3, [(more...)](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/figure/A1062/?report=objectonly)

This model is undoubtedly an oversimplification, because different cells use this pathway in different ways (see [Cox and Peifer 1998](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/#)). Moreover, its components can have more than one function in the cell. In addition to being part of the Wnt signal transduction cascade, GSK-3 is also a metabolic enzyme regulating glycogen metabolism. The β-catenin protein was first recognized as being part of the cell adhesion complex on the cell surface before it was also found to be a transcription factor. The APC protein also functions as a tumor suppressor in adults. The transformation of normal colon cells into colon cancer is thought to occur when the APC gene is mutated and can no longer keep the β-catenin protein out of the nucleus ([Figure 6.23B](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/figure/A1062/?report=objectonly); [Korinek et al. 1997](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/#); [He et al. 1998](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/#)). Once in the nucleus, β-catenin can bind with another transcription factor and activate genes for cell division.

One principle that is readily seen in the Wnt pathway (and which is also evident in the Hedgehog pathway) is that activation is often accomplished by inhibiting an inhibitor. Thus, the GSK-3 protein is an inhibitor that is itself repressed by the Wnt signal.

[Go to:](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/# "Go to other sections in this page")

The Hedgehog pathway
--------------------

Members of the Hedgehog protein family function by binding to a receptor called Patched. The Patched protein, however, is not a signal transducer. Rather, it is bound to a signal transducer, the Smoothened protein. The Patched protein prevents the Smoothened protein from functioning. In the absence of Hedgehog binding to Patched, the Smoothened protein is inactive, and the Cubitus interruptus (Ci) protein is tethered to the microtubules of the responding cell. While on the microtubules, it is cleaved in such a way that a portion of it enters the nucleus and acts as a transcriptional repressor. This portion of the Ci protein binds to the promoters and enhancers of particular genes and acts as an inhibitor of transcription. When Hedgehog binds to the Patched protein, the Patched protein's shape is altered such that it no longer inhibits Smoothened. The Smoothened protein acts (probably by phosphorylation) to release the Ci protein from the microtubules and to prevent its being cleaved. The intact Ci protein can now enter the nucleus, where it acts as a transcriptional _activator_ of the same genes it used to repress ([Figure 6.24](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/figure/A1064/?report=objectonly); [Aza-Blanc et al. 1997](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/#)).

[![Image 14: Figure 6.24. The Hedgehog signal transduction pathway.](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/bin/ch6f24.gif)](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/figure/A1064/?report=objectonly "Figure 6.24")

#### [Figure 6.24](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/figure/A1064/?report=objectonly)

The Hedgehog signal transduction pathway. The Patched protein in the cell membrane is an inhibitor of the Smoothened protein. (A) In the absence of Hedgehog binding to Patched, the Ci protein is tethered to the microtubules (by the Cos2 and Fused proteins). [(more...)](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/figure/A1064/?report=objectonly)

The Hedgehog pathway is extremely important in limb and neural differentiation in vertebrates. When mice were made homozygous for a mutant allele of Sonic hedgehog, they had major limb abnormalities as well as cyclopia—a single eye in the center of the forehead ([Chiang et al. 1996](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/#)). The vertebrate homologues of the Ci protein in _Drosophila_ are the Gli proteins. Severe truncations of the human _GLI3_ gene produce a nonfunctional protein that gives rise to Grieg cephalopoly-syndactyly, a condition involving a high forehead and extra digits. A less severe truncation retains the DNA binding domain of the GLI3 protein, but deletes the activion region. Thus, this mutant GLI3 protein can act only as a repressor. This protein is found in patients with Pallister-Hall syndrome, a much more severe syndrome (indeed, lethal soon after birth) involving not only extra digits, but also poor development of the pituitary gland, hypothalamus, anus, and kidneys (see [Shin et al. 1999](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/#)). While mutations that inactivate the Hedgehog pathway can cause malformations, mutations that activate the pathway ectopically can cause cancers. If the Patched protein is mutated in somatic tissues such that it can no longer inhibit Smoothened, it can cause basal cell carcinomas, tumors of the basal cell layer of the epidermis. Heritable mutations of the _patched_ gene cause basal cell nevus syndrome, a rare autosomal dominant condition characterized by both developmental anomalies (fused fingers, rib and facial abnormalities) and multiple malignant tumors such as basal cell carcinoma ([Hahn et al. 1996](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/#); [Johnson et al. 1996](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/#)).

One remarkable feature of the Hedgehog signal transduction pathway is the importance of cholesterol. First, cholesterol is critical for the catalytic cleavage of Sonic hedgehog protein. Only the amino-terminal portion of the protein is functional and secreted. Second, the Patched protein that binds the Sonic hedgehog protein also needs cholesterol in order to function. It has recently been found ([Kelley et al. 1996](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/#); [Roessler et al. 1996](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/#)) that some human cyclopia syndromes are caused by mutations in genes that encode either Sonic hedgehog or the enzymes that synthesize cholesterol. Moreover, certain chemicals that induce cyclopia do so by interfering with the cholesterol biosynthetic enzymes ([Figure 6.25](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/figure/A1065/?report=objectonly); Beachy et al. 1997; [Cooper et al. 1998](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/#)). Environmental factors that cause developmental anomalies are called **teratogens** (from the Greek, meaning “monster-former”), and they will discussed in more detail in Chapter 21. Two teratogens known to cause cyclopia in vertebrates are jervine and cyclopamine. Both substances are found in the plant _Veratrum californicum_ ([Keeler and Binns et al. 1968](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/#)), and both block the synthesis of cholesterol.

[![Image 15: Figure 6.25. Head of a cyclopic lamb born of a ewe who had eaten Veratrum californicum early in pregnancy.](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/bin/ch6f25.gif)](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/figure/A1065/?report=objectonly "Figure 6.25")

#### [Figure 6.25](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/figure/A1065/?report=objectonly)

Head of a cyclopic lamb born of a ewe who had eaten _Veratrum californicum_ early in pregnancy. The cerebral hemispheres fused, forming only one central eye and no pituitary gland. The jervine alkaloid made by this plant inhibits cholesterol synthesis, [(more...)](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/figure/A1065/?report=objectonly)

[![Image 16: Box Icon](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/corehtml/pmc/css/bookshelf/2.26/img/box-icon.gif)](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10043/box/A1066/?report=objectonly "Box")

enriched_points.expanded_content: Cell-surface receptors, such as GPCRs and RTKs, facilitate signal transduction by binding ligands, triggering conformational changes that activate intracellular signaling cascades.
Intracellular receptors, including nuclear receptors, bind hydrophobic ligands like steroid hormones, leading to gene expression modulation upon their translocation into the nucleus.
Receptors are vital for signal transduction, orchestrating cellular responses and influencing processes like growth, differentiation, and apoptosis through complex signaling networks.
